sentences.base_language: Early one Saturday morning, Camille decided to go to the local market.
sentences.target_language: Un samedi matin de bonne heure, Camille a décidé d'aller au marché du quartier.
sentences.grammarNote:
**De bonne heure** = early — a fixed expression: partir **de bonne heure** (leave early), arriver **de bonne heure** (arrive early). More formal/literary than **tôt** (early): se lever **tôt** (get up early) vs partir **de bonne heure** (set off early). **De bonne heure** carries a slightly deliberate, purposeful feel — you've planned to be up early.
**Décider de + infinitif** = to decide to do: a décidé **d'**aller (decided to go). The **de** before the infinitive is required after **décider**: **décider de** faire, **décider de** partir. Compare: **décider que** + indicatif (decide that — reporting a decision about a fact): elle a décidé **qu'**elle resterait (she decided that she would stay).

sentences.base_language: She had always loved markets — the colors, the smells, the atmosphere.
sentences.target_language: Elle avait toujours adoré les marchés — les couleurs, les odeurs, l'ambiance.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avait adoré** — plus-que-parfait: **avait** (imparfait de avoir) + adoré (participe passé). The plus-que-parfait here expresses a long-standing preference that pre-existed the morning's story — she had always loved markets (before and up to now). **Adorer** + noun: **adorer les** marchés (love markets). **Adorer** is stronger than **aimer** — use it for genuine passion.
**L'ambiance** (f.) = the atmosphere, the vibe — from **ambiant** (surrounding): l'ambiance **du** marché (the market atmosphere). Compare: **l'atmosphère** (atmosphere — more general, scientific or figurative), **l'ambiance** (atmosphere — specifically the feel/mood of a place or social setting). **L'ambiance** is very common in everyday French: quelle bonne **ambiance** ici ! (what a great vibe here!).

sentences.base_language: She got dressed quickly and had a coffee and a croissant before leaving.
sentences.target_language: Elle s'est habillée rapidement et a pris un café et un croissant avant de partir.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'est habillée** — passé composé of a pronominal verb with **être**: the past participle agrees with the subject: s'est habill**ée** (feminine). Compare: il s'est habill**é** (masculine). **Prendre** + food/drink = to have (eat or drink): **prendre** un café (have a coffee), **prendre** un repas (have a meal), **prendre** le petit-déjeuner (have breakfast). **Prendre** is more versatile and natural than **boire** or **manger** in many food/drink contexts.
**Avant de + infinitif** = before doing (same subject): **avant de** partir (before leaving). When subjects differ, use **avant que** + subjonctif: **avant qu'**il parte (before he leaves). This rule — **avant de** + infinitif (same subject) vs **avant que** + subjonctif (different subjects) — also applies to **pour** (pour + inf vs pour que + subj), **sans** (sans + inf vs sans que + subj), **afin de** (afin de + inf vs afin que + subj).

sentences.base_language: The market was only a ten-minute walk from her apartment.
sentences.target_language: Le marché n'était qu'à dix minutes à pied de son appartement.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ne… que** = only — the restrictive negation: **n'était qu'à** dix minutes (was only ten minutes away). **Ne… que** is used to express 'only' with a verb: il **ne** mange **que** des légumes (he eats only vegetables). Compare: **seulement** (only — adverb, used without ne): il mange **seulement** des légumes. **Ne… que** is more formal; **seulement** more colloquial. Both are correct.
**À + time + à pied** = a ~ walk away: **à dix minutes à pied** (ten minutes on foot). **À pied** (on foot) is the standard expression: aller **à pied** (go on foot). Compare modes of transport: **en voiture** (by car), **en métro** (by subway), **à vélo** (by bike) — note **à** is used for bike and foot; **en** for most vehicles. This **à/en** distinction with transport is important.

sentences.base_language: When she arrived, the stalls were already overflowing with fresh produce.
sentences.target_language: À son arrivée, les étals débordaient déjà de produits frais.
sentences.grammarNote:
**À son arrivée** = upon her arrival — **à** + **son** + verbal noun: upon her arrival (= when she arrived). This is a elegant alternative to **quand elle est arrivée** (when she arrived). **À + noun of action**: **à son départ** (upon her departure), **à sa lecture** (upon reading it), **à l'annonce** (upon the announcement). Mastering this construction gives writing a formal, polished quality.
**Étal** (m.) = market stall, display counter — the word for the table or surface where goods are displayed at a market. Plural: **étals** or **étaux** (less common). Compare: **stand** (m., stand — more modern/fair context), **stand** (trade fair), **étal** (traditional market). **Déborder de** = to overflow with, to be overflowing with: la salle **débordait de** monde (the room was overflowing with people), **déborder de** joie (to overflow with joy — figurative).

sentences.base_language: A cheese seller was enthusiastically describing his products to passersby.
sentences.target_language: Un fromager décrivait avec enthousiasme ses produits aux passants.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Décrivait** — imparfait for an ongoing scene: the cheese seller was describing (an activity in progress when Camille arrived). The imparfait sets the background: **décrivait** (was describing — scene) while the preterit would advance the plot: il **décrivit** (he described — literary past). In modern spoken French, the passé composé advances the plot; the imparfait describes the background scene.
**Fromager/ère** (m./f.) = cheese maker, cheese seller — from **fromage** (cheese). The **-ger/-ière** suffix forms profession nouns from food names: **fromager** (cheese person), **boulanger** (baker, from **pain** via Old French bole), **boucher** (butcher). **Passant/e** = passer-by — present participle of **passer** used as a noun: **les passants** (the passersby). The same pattern: **habitant** (inhabitant), **commerçant** (merchant, trader).

sentences.base_language: Camille asked him: "Do you have any goat's cheese?"
sentences.target_language: Camille lui a demandé : « Avez-vous du fromage de chèvre ? »
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lui a demandé** — indirect object **lui** (to him): **demander** à quelqu'un takes an indirect object for the person asked: **lui** a demandé (asked him/her — never *le/la* for demander). The question itself is the direct object (or introduced by **si** for yes/no questions): elle **lui** a demandé **si** il avait du fromage (she asked him whether he had any cheese).
**Du fromage de chèvre** — partitive **du**: **du** fromage de chèvre (some goat's cheese — an unspecified quantity). The partitive article (**du/de la/de l'/des**) expresses 'some' before uncountable or unspecified quantities. After negation, partitives become **de**: vous **n'**avez **pas de** fromage de chèvre (you don't have any goat's cheese). **De chèvre** = of goat / goat's: de + animal = type: fromage de **brebis** (sheep's cheese), fromage de **vache** (cow's cheese).

sentences.base_language: He smiled and said: "But of course! Try this one — it's very fresh."
sentences.target_language: Il a souri et a dit : « Bien sûr ! Goûtez celui-ci — il est très frais. »
sentences.grammarNote:
**Goûtez** — impératif of **goûter** (vous form): **goûtez** (taste it — polite imperative). The vous-imperative = present indicative vous form minus **vous**: vous goûtez → **goûtez**. Very useful in service contexts: **goûtez** (taste this), **essayez** (try this), **regardez** (look at this).
**Celui-ci** = this one (close) — demonstrative pronoun: **celui-ci** (this one — m. singular), **celle-ci** (this one — f. singular), **ceux-ci** (these — m. plural), **celles-ci** (these — f. plural). The **-ci** (here) vs **-là** (there) distinguishes proximity: **celui-ci** (this one) vs **celui-là** (that one). **Celui** alone cannot stand alone in modern French — it needs **-ci/-là** or a relative clause: **celui** que j'ai acheté (the one I bought).

sentences.base_language: She bought a piece of cheese, a bunch of radishes, and some olives.
sentences.target_language: Elle a acheté un morceau de fromage, une botte de radis et des olives.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Botte de** = a bunch of (vegetables): **une botte de** radis (a bunch of radishes), **une botte de** persil (a bunch of parsley), **une botte de** carottes (a bunch of carrots). A **botte** is a bundle tied together — distinct from **bouquet** (bouquet — for flowers) and **botte** meaning boot (feminine, same spelling, different meaning — context distinguishes). Learning **botte** is essential for French market vocabulary.
**Des olives** — partitive plural: **des** olives (some olives — unspecified quantity). In plural contexts, the partitive **des** is the default: **des** légumes, **des** fruits, **des** olives. After negation: pas **d'**olives (not *pas des olives* in standard French). After expressions of quantity: un kilo **d'**olives, une boîte **d'**olives (note the **de** not **des** after a quantity expression).

sentences.base_language: At the fruit stall, she tried a slice of cantaloupe and found it wonderfully sweet.
sentences.target_language: Au stand de fruits, elle a goûté un morceau de melon et l'a trouvé merveilleusement sucré.
sentences.grammarNote:
**L'a trouvé merveilleusement sucré** = found it wonderfully sweet: **trouver** + direct object + adjective = to find ~ (to be): elle **l'**a **trouvé** sucré (she found it sweet). The direct object pronoun **l'** (it — referring to **le melon**, masculine) precedes the auxiliary. The adjective **sucré** agrees with **le melon** (masculine): **sucré** (m.). Compare: **trouver** que + clause (find that — less direct): elle a **trouvé que** c'était sucré.
**Merveilleusement** = wonderfully, marvelously — adverb from **merveilleux/merveilleuse** (wonderful). The pattern for adverbs from adjectives ending in **-eux/-euse**: take the feminine form and add **-ment**: **heureusement** (happily, fortunately), **merveilleusement** (wonderfully), **doucement** (gently — from **doux/douce**). French adverbs of manner formed from adjectives are a key area of vocabulary expansion.

sentences.base_language: The seller offered her another slice to take home.
sentences.target_language: Le vendeur lui a offert une autre tranche à emporter.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Offrir à quelqu'un** = to offer someone something: le vendeur **lui** a offert (the seller offered her — **lui** = indirect object 'to her'). **Offrir** is an irregular verb: **offrir** → j'offre, j'ai **offert** (passé composé). Pattern like **couvrir, souffrir, ouvrir**: j'**ouvre**, j'ai **ouvert** — the past participle ends in **-ert**. **Une autre tranche** = another slice: **une autre** + noun is the standard way to say 'another' in French — never *un autre de*.
**À emporter** = to take away, to go: **à emporter** (for takeaway). This infinitive phrase expresses purpose/manner: une tranche **à emporter** (a slice to take home). Compare: **sur place** (to eat in/on site — the opposite of à emporter): manger **sur place** (eat in) vs commander **à emporter** (order to go). **À emporter** is now used for any takeaway food.

sentences.base_language: Her arms were getting heavy from carrying all the shopping bags.
sentences.target_language: Ses bras commençaient à peser sous le poids de tous ses sacs.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Commencer à + infinitif** = to begin to, to start doing: **commençaient à** peser (were starting to feel heavy). **Commencer à** takes the infinitive directly: **commencer à** travailler (start working), **commencer à** pleuvoir (start raining). Compare: **commencer par** + infinitif (start by doing — the first step in a sequence): il a **commencé par** se présenter (he started by introducing himself). **Commencer à** vs **commencer par** is an important distinction.
**Peser** intransitively = to weigh, to feel heavy: les sacs **pesaient** (the bags were heavy). **Peser** as an intransitive verb describes the subjective feeling of weight: les paupières me **pesaient** (my eyelids felt heavy). Compare: **peser** transitively = to weigh (something): **peser** les légumes (weigh the vegetables). The same verb, different syntax. **Sous le poids de** = under the weight of: **sous le poids** du passé (under the weight of the past — figurative).

sentences.base_language: She suddenly ran into her former neighbor, who was buying flowers.
sentences.target_language: Elle est tombée sur son ancienne voisine qui achetait des fleurs.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tomber sur quelqu'un** = to run into / to come across someone unexpectedly: elle est **tombée sur** son ancienne voisine (she ran into her former neighbor). **Tomber sur** uses **être** in the passé composé: elle **est** tombée (not *a tombé* — for this intransitive use). Note: **tomber** + **sur** = to come across, while **tomber** + **à/dans** = to fall into. **Tomber sur** for unexpected encounters is very colloquial and natural.
**Ancienne voisine** — **ancien/ne** before a noun = former: son **ancienne** voisine (her former neighbor). **Ancien** before a noun = former/past: **ancien** président (former president), mon **ancienne** école (my former school). **Ancien** after a noun = ancient/old: une ville **ancienne** (an ancient city), un meuble **ancien** (an antique piece of furniture). This position-dependent meaning change is a key feature of French adjectives.

sentences.base_language: They stopped to chat and agreed to have coffee together the following week.
sentences.target_language: Elles se sont arrêtées pour bavarder et se sont donné rendez-vous la semaine suivante.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'arrêter pour + infinitif** = to stop in order to do: **se sont arrêtées pour** bavarder (stopped to chat). **Pour** + infinitif expresses purpose. Compare: **arrêter de + infinitif** (to stop doing — cease): elles ont **arrêté de** travailler (they stopped working). **S'arrêter pour** = stop in order to; **arrêter de** = cease doing.
**Se donner rendez-vous** = to make/arrange a meeting with each other: elles **se sont donné** rendez-vous (they arranged to meet — reciprocal). The past participle **donné** does not agree here because **rendez-vous** is the direct object (given to each other) and precedes the verb only by the reflexive, not by a direct object pronoun. This is a nuance of past participle agreement with **avoir** verbs in pronominal constructions.

sentences.base_language: She bought some sourdough bread at a small artisan bakery.
sentences.target_language: Elle a acheté du pain au levain dans une petite boulangerie artisanale.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Pain au levain** = sourdough bread — **levain** (sourdough starter) + **au** (with/of): bread made with sourdough. French bread vocabulary is specific: **pain au levain** (sourdough), **pain de campagne** (country loaf), **pain complet** (whole grain bread), **baguette** (long thin bread), **ficelle** (even thinner baguette). Knowing these names is essential for navigating any French boulangerie.
**Artisanal/e** = artisanal, handcrafted — from **artisan** (craftsperson). An important word in modern French food culture: **boulangerie artisanale** (artisan bakery — made by hand, traditional methods), **fromage artisanal** (artisan cheese). **Artisanal** is used to distinguish traditional, handmade products from industrial ones. Compare: **industriel** (industrial — mass-produced), **artisanal** (handcrafted — small-scale, traditional).

sentences.base_language: The warm smell of fresh bread made her stomach growl with hunger.
sentences.target_language: L'odeur chaude du pain frais lui a fait gargouiller l'estomac.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Faire + infinitif** = to make/cause something to happen: l'odeur lui a **fait** gargouiller l'estomac (the smell made her stomach growl — lit. made the stomach gurgle to her). **Faire** + infinitif is the causative construction: le bruit **fait** vibrer les murs (the noise makes the walls vibrate), ça **fait** rire tout le monde (it makes everyone laugh). The subject of the infinitive either follows it (when no direct object) or is expressed as indirect object pronoun.
**Gargouiller** = to gurgle, to growl (of a stomach) — **l'estomac** gargouille (the stomach growls). A very onomatopoeic French verb: les canalisations **gargoullent** (the pipes gurgle). Compare: **gronder** (to growl/rumble — more general), **gargouiller** (specifically the liquid-like gurgling sound of hunger). **L'odeur chaude** = the warm smell — **chaud/e** applied to a smell is a synesthetic expression (warm = rising, enveloping, fresh-baked).

sentences.base_language: She also lingered at a stall selling handmade pottery and ceramics.
sentences.target_language: Elle s'est aussi attardée devant un stand de poteries et céramiques artisanales.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'attarder** = to linger, to spend more time than intended: elle **s'est attardée** devant le stand (she lingered in front of the stall). **S'attarder** (pronominal, uses **être**): agreement — **s'est attardée** (feminine). **S'attarder** implies you stayed longer than planned or expected: ne **t'**attarde pas (don't linger/dawdle). Compare: **rester** (to stay — neutral), **s'attarder** (to linger — implies more time than intended).
**Poterie** vs **céramique**: **poterie** (pottery — broader, includes earthenware, terracotta); **céramique** (ceramic — often higher-end or more artistic). In everyday speech, the terms overlap. **Devant** = in front of, before: **devant** un stand (in front of a stall). **Devant** is used both literally (physically in front) and figuratively: **devant** la difficulté (faced with the difficulty). Compare: **face à** (facing, confronting — more figurative challenge).

sentences.base_language: By midday, her feet and shoulders ached from all the walking and carrying.
sentences.target_language: Vers midi, elle avait mal aux pieds et aux épaules à force de marcher et de porter.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avoir mal à + article + body part** = to have pain in ~: **avait mal aux** pieds (her feet ached), **aux** épaules (her shoulders). **Aux** = **à + les** (mandatory contraction before plural nouns). Compare singular: **avait mal au** dos (back — **au** = **à + le**), **avait mal à la** tête (head — no contraction, feminine), **avait mal à l'**estomac (stomach — elision before vowel).
**À force de + infinitif/nom** = from so much doing, by dint of: **à force de** marcher et de porter (from so much walking and carrying). This construction expresses a result caused by repeated or sustained action: **à force de** travailler, il est épuisé (from working so much, he's exhausted), **à force de** patience (by dint of patience). The infinitive after **à force de** requires **de** before each verb when listing: **à force de** marcher **et de** porter (not *marcher et porter*).

sentences.base_language: She sat down at a little café at the edge of the market and ordered a mint tea.
sentences.target_language: Elle s'est installée dans un petit café en bordure du marché et a commandé un thé à la menthe.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'installer** = to settle in, to make oneself comfortable — more deliberate than **s'asseoir** (to sit down): elle **s'est installée** (she settled in comfortably). **S'installer** implies finding a spot and making oneself at home: **s'installer** dans un fauteuil (settle into an armchair), **s'installer** en terrasse (sit out on the terrace). Compare: **s'asseoir** (to sit down — the physical act), **s'installer** (to settle in — implies comfort and intention to stay).
**En bordure de** = at the edge of, on the border of: **en bordure du** marché (at the edge of the market). Compare: **au bord de** (at the edge/shore of — often for water or physical edges): **au bord de** la rivière (at the river's edge), **au bord du** gouffre (on the brink of the abyss). **En bordure de** is more neutral and works well for roads, markets, and zones. **Thé à la menthe** = mint tea — a very popular drink in French cafés, influenced by North African culture.

sentences.base_language: She arrived home content, bags full of beautiful things and a heart full of happiness.
sentences.target_language: Elle est rentrée chez elle ravie, les sacs chargés de belles choses et le cœur léger.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Rentrée chez elle** — **rentrer** + **être** auxiliary: **rentrer** (to come/go back home) uses **être** in compound tenses: elle **est** rentrée (she came home). **Rentrer** specifically means 'to go back home' — compare **retourner** (to go back to a place, not necessarily home) and **revenir** (to come back — toward speaker). **Rentrée** agrees with subject: **rentrée** (feminine).
**Le cœur léger** = with a light heart, feeling light-hearted — **léger/ère** (light) applied to the heart is a common fixed expression in French: partir **le cœur léger** (leave light-hearted). The structure [article] + [body part/abstract noun] + [adjective] is a French way of expressing an emotional state or manner: **les mains vides** (empty-handed), **la tête haute** (with head held high), **le cœur lourd** (heavy-hearted — the opposite). **Ravie** = delighted, thrilled — **ravi/e** (from **ravir**, to delight/enrapture): a stronger and warmer word than **contente** (pleased).
